title: Quiz: Code Distance and Error Thresholds

questions.question: What is the code distance of a d×d rotated planar surface code?
questions.explanation: The code distance equals d, the lattice dimension. This is because the minimum weight logical operator (either X_L or Z_L) requires d physical operations to span from one boundary to its opposite.

questions.question: A distance-7 surface code can correct up to how many errors?
questions.explanation: A distance-d code can correct up to t = ⌊(d-1)/2⌋ errors. For d=7: t = ⌊6/2⌋ = 3 errors. With 3 or fewer errors, the decoder can always find the correct recovery.

questions.question: What is the approximate threshold error rate for surface codes under circuit-level noise?
questions.explanation: The surface code has a threshold of approximately 0.8-1.1% under circuit-level noise (including gate, measurement, and idle errors). This high threshold is one of the main advantages of surface codes.

questions.question: Below threshold, how does the logical error rate scale with distance?
questions.explanation: Below threshold, the logical error rate is exponentially suppressed with distance: p_L ∝ (p/p_th)^((d+1)/2) ≈ exp(-α×d). This exponential suppression is why increasing distance is so effective.

questions.question: What happens when the physical error rate exceeds the threshold?

options.text: Error correction still works but more slowly
The logical error rate approaches 50% regardless of distance
Only X errors can be corrected

questions.explanation: Above threshold, increasing the code distance actually makes things worse, and the logical error rate approaches 50% (random guessing). Error correction provides no benefit when p > p_th.

questions.question: How many total physical qubits (data + ancilla) are needed for a distance-5 surface code?
questions.explanation: A distance-5 code has d² = 25 data qubits and approximately d² - 1 = 24 ancilla qubits (split between X and Z types), for a total of about 49 physical qubits.

questions.question: To reduce the logical error rate by a factor of 10, you typically need to increase the distance by about 2.
questions.explanation: True. Due to the exponential scaling p_L ∝ (p/p_th)^((d+1)/2), increasing d by 2 roughly squares the suppression factor, which typically gives about 10× improvement in logical error rate (depends on p/p_th ratio).
